name: Goal-Directed vs Stimulus-Driven Attention

source.author: Maurizio Corbetta, Gordon L. Shulman
source.paper: Control of goal-directed and stimulus-driven attention in the brain

biological_basis: Two attention networks: dorsal frontoparietal (goal-directed, top-down) and ventral temporoparietal (stimulus-driven, bottom-up salience). They interact to select behaviourally relevant information.
core_claim: Attention is controlled by both internal goals (what you seek) and external salience (what surprises you). Effective selection requires balancing both signals.

implementable_mechanism.description: Compute salience as weighted combination of bottom-up surprise (deviation from expected) and top-down goal relevance (similarity to current goal vector). Route highest-salience signals to workspace.
implementable_mechanism.pseudocode: for s in signals: surprise = distance(s, expected); relevance = similarity(s, goal); salience = alpha*surprise + beta*relevance; return sort(signals, by=salience)

notes: Direct implementation in AttentionGate with alpha=0.6, beta=0.4.

name: Biased Competition Model

source.author: Robert Desimone, John Duncan
source.paper: Neural mechanisms of selective visual attention

biological_basis: Multiple stimuli within a neuron's receptive field compete for representation. Top-down bias from prefrontal cortex shifts competition in favour of task-relevant stimuli, suppressing irrelevant ones.
core_claim: Attention works by biasing competitive interactions — not amplifying targets alone but suppressing distractors through lateral inhibition and top-down bias.

implementable_mechanism.description: Limited workspace slots create competition. Signals compete via salience scores; losers are suppressed (not admitted). Top-down goal vector biases competition toward relevant content.
implementable_mechanism.pseudocode: scores = [bias.dot(r) - inhibition(r, competitors) for r in reps]; winners = top_k(scores, capacity); suppressed = rest

notes: Informs workspace eviction policy: lowest salience*age loses competition.
